text: The mission of Jesus in Judea (Jn 3:22–36) is not often a focus in modern Jesus studies, yet it is profoundly significant for the geography of the Gospel of John. This paper examines the spatial dynamics of John 2:13–4:54 and explores the possibility that this section organises the mission of Jesus within a spatial dynamics that echoes the missional structure of Acts 1:8. Specifically, the geography of the Gospel of John organises the mission of Jesus by moving progressively from the centre (Jerusalem) to the periphery (Judea, Samaria and Galilee), so that all parts of Israel hear the gospel before the tension with the Jewish authorities that characterises the second part of the Gospel is introduced. Furthermore, the use of the term 'Jew' in John 3:25, in relation to the dispute between the Judean disciples and those of John the Baptist, also becomes significant when one realises that 'land of Judea' functions as the spatial referent in this context means that the term Ἰουδαῖος in John 3:25 is not necessarily an ethnic term but a regional term for a Judean, a term that in this context organises the episode into the mission of Jesus to all of Israel in a way that illuminates the scripture.
